---
title: "Organising GitHub for Reproducibility<br>and Lab Onboarding"
subtitle: "Designing repositories for future humans · GitHub Training Series"
author: "Patrícia Rodrigues · UK DRI @ Cardiff"
date: "26 March 2026"
format:
  ukdri-revealjs:
    smaller: true
---

## Today's session {background-color="#00326e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**By the end of this hour you will:**

- Understand why repo structure matters for reproducibility
- Know the minimal elements of a well-organised research repository
- Have a template you can copy for your next project
- Know how to use GitHub to onboard new lab members

**No Git commands today.**
This session is about design, not tooling.
:::
:::


---

## Scope — what we will and won't cover

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**We will cover**

- Repository organisation and structure
- A minimal reproducible project template
- Writing documentation for onboarding
- Practical conventions for research teams
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**We will not cover**

- Git basics or command line
- Branching strategies
- Continuous integration / automation
- Advanced GitHub workflows

*Those topics are covered in other sessions — links at the end.*
:::
:::


---

## Part 1 · The Problem {background-color="#00326e"}

---

## What a typical research repo looks like

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```
my_project/
├── analysis_final.R
├── analysis_final_v2.R
├── analysis_FINAL_USE_THIS.R
├── data.csv
├── data_old.csv
├── data2.csv
├── figures/
│   ├── plot1.png
│   ├── plot_new.png
│   └── plot_for_paper.png
├── scratch.R
└── notes.txt
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What does this repo say to a new starter?**

. . .

- Which file should I run?
- Which data is current?
- What do the figures mean?
- Where do I put my own work?

. . .

*Nobody can answer these questions when the original author has left.*
:::
:::


---

## The real cost

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**When a new person joins:**

- Spends days figuring out the project structure
- Asks questions the PI doesn't have time to answer
- Makes a copy of the wrong file and introduces silent errors

. . .

**When someone leaves:**

- Knowledge walks out the door with them
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
> "It worked when I ran it."

> "The person who set this up left in January."

> "I think the data is in a folder on their old laptop."

*These are not edge cases.*
:::
:::


---

## The real cost, six months later

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**When you come back after 6 months:**

- Can't remember which script does what
- Can't reproduce a figure from a paper
- Lose time re-learning your own work
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
**What this really means:**

- Delays for new starters
- Repeated questions for senior staff
- Fragile projects that depend on memory

. . .

*Poor structure is a time cost, not just a tidy-up problem.*
:::
:::


---

## The key idea

> *"Your most important collaborator is your future self six months from now."*

. . .

**Reproducibility and onboarding are the same problem.**

. . .

Both are about **handing work to someone else**.

Often that "someone else" is **future-you**.

. . .

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Reproducibility** means:

Someone else can take your work and get the same result.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Onboarding** means:

Someone new can understand your work and continue it.
:::
:::

. . .

**The solution to both is the same: a well-designed repository.**


---

## Part 2 · Three Principles {background-color="#00326e"}

---

## Principle 1 · Structure beats memory

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
You cannot rely on people remembering how a project works.

- The PI is busy
- The original postdoc has left
- Even you will forget in six months

. . .

**A clear directory structure makes a project understandable even without the author.**

The structure itself communicates:

- Where things live
- What belongs where
- What the project is doing
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
**Compare:**

A repo where you have to ask someone what to run.

vs.

A repo where you open it and immediately see `README.md`, `03_data/`, `04_data_analysis/`, `05_figures/`.

. . .

*One of these is a gift to future collaborators.*
:::
:::


---

## Principle 2 · One project ≠ one person

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
Research repositories are often built as if only one person will ever use them.

- No README
- No explanation of how to run the analysis
- Folder names that only make sense to the author

. . .

**Repositories should be designed for collaboration and handover, not individual workflows.**

Even if you work alone today, you will eventually:

- Have a new student join
- Collaborate with someone from outside your group
- Hand the project to someone else
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
**Ask yourself:**

*Could someone who doesn't know me get started in this repository with no help from me?*

. . .

If the answer is no — that's the problem to fix.
:::
:::


---

## Principle 3 · Onboarding is part of reproducibility

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
Reproducibility is often framed as a technical problem:

- Use version control
- Record your environment
- Write clean code

. . .

**If a new researcher cannot tell how to start, the project is not truly reproducible.**

Onboarding documentation is part of reproducibility, not optional extra work.
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
**Onboarding documentation answers:**

- What is this project?
- How do I run it?
- Where do I put my own work?
- Who do I ask if I have a question?
- What are the conventions we use here?

*A 30-minute investment in writing this saves days of confusion later.*
:::
:::


---

## Part 3 · The Minimal Template {background-color="#00326e"}

---

## The smallest structure that works

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```
lab-repo/
├── README.md
├── docs/
│   └── onboarding.md
└── projects/
    └── project-template/
        ├── README.md
        ├── 01-Project_management/
        ├── 02_material_methods/
        ├── 03_data/
        │   ├── 001_rawdata/
        │   └── 990_processed_data/
        ├── 04_data_analysis/
        ├── 05_figures/
        ├── 06_dissemination/
        └── 07_miscellaneous/
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Three things every lab repo needs:**

1. A **README** — what this is and how to start
2. An **onboarding doc** — expectations and conventions
3. A **project template** — a consistent GIN-Tonic-style structure for each analysis

. . .

*That's it. You don't need more than this to start.*
:::
:::


---

## `README.md` — the front door

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**The README is the first file anyone reads.**

It should answer:

- What is this repository?
- Who maintains it?
- How do I get started?
- Where do I find things?

. . .

**A README does not need to be long.**

Four sections. Plain English. No jargon.

The template has a fill-in-the-blanks structure — you just replace the placeholders.
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Minimal README structure:**

```markdown
# Lab Name — Research Repository

Short description of the lab and
what this repository is for.

## Getting started

Point to onboarding.md.

## Repository structure

Brief explanation of the
top-level folders.

## Contact

Who to ask for help.
```
:::
:::


---

## `docs/onboarding.md` — the welcome guide

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**The onboarding document is for new people joining the lab.**

It should explain:

- What we work on (the science context)
- How we use this repository
- Conventions we follow (naming files, committing, reviewing)
- Where to ask questions

. . .

**Write it as if you're talking to someone on their first day.**

Not as formal documentation — as a friendly guide.
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Key sections:**

- Welcome and context
- How to get set up
- Repository conventions
- Who to contact for what

. . .

*If you find yourself re-explaining the same things to every new starter — that belongs in this file.*
:::
:::


---

## `projects/project-template/` — the analysis scaffold

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```
project-template/
├── README.md
├── 01-Project_management/
├── 02_material_methods/
├── 03_data/
│   ├── 001_rawdata/        ← never modify this
│   └── 990_processed_data/ ← generated by scripts
├── 04_data_analysis/       ← analysis code and notebooks
├── 05_figures/             ← generated outputs to share
├── 06_dissemination/
└── 07_miscellaneous/       ← environment files and extras
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Key conventions:**

- Keep the GIN-Tonic top-level numbering unchanged
- `03_data/001_rawdata/` is **read-only** — never overwrite source data
- Analysis code lives in `04_data_analysis/`
- Use the same numbering for analysis steps: `001_cleaning.R`, `002_model.R`
- Generated outputs go in `05_figures/` or `03_data/990_processed_data/`
- Environment files can live in `07_miscellaneous/`

. . .

*Copy this folder for every new project. Give it a meaningful name.*
:::
:::


---

## Setting up the structure — two ways

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Option A — Copy on GitHub**

1. Open `https://github.com/UKDRI/ukdri-repro-template`
2. Click **Use this template**
3. Create a new repository from the template
4. Rename and adapt it for your project

*No command line needed.*

. . .

**Works well for:** people new to the terminal, or one-off projects.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Option B — One script**

```bash
bash new-project.sh my-analysis-name
```

Creates everything instantly:

```
my-analysis-name/
├── README.md
├── 01-Project_management/
├── 02_material_methods/
├── 03_data/
│   ├── 001_rawdata/
│   └── 990_processed_data/
├── 04_data_analysis/
├── 05_figures/
├── 06_dissemination/
└── 07_miscellaneous/
```

*Script is in the template repo. Run it once per project.*
:::
:::


---

## Environment capture — where it goes

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**"It worked on my machine"** is one of the most common reproducibility failures.

In this template, environment files live in `07_miscellaneous/` so they are easy to find and keep with the project.

. . .

**What goes here depends on your tools:**

| Tool | File |
|------|------|
| R | `renv.lock` (from `renv`) |
| Python | `requirements.txt` or `environment.yml` |
| R + Python | Both |
| Containers | `Dockerfile` |

. . .

**You don't need all of these.** Start with the one that fits your workflow.
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Minimum viable environment capture:**

For R:
```r
# Run once when you set up the project
install.packages("renv")
renv::init()
```

For Python:
```bash
# Save your current packages
pip freeze > requirements.txt
```

. . .

*Record this early. Retrofitting environment capture is painful.*
:::
:::


---

## Best practices that prevent chaos

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Naming and file conventions**

- Number analysis steps clearly: `001_cleaning.R`, `002_qc.R`, `003_model.R`
- Use one naming style consistently: lowercase with underscores is usually safest
- Use descriptive names, not `final_v2_really_final.R`
- Avoid spaces and special characters in file names
- Keep one idea per file name: `manhattan_plot.png`, not `plot_new_latest.png`
- Keep dates in `YYYY-MM-DD` format when you need dated outputs
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Documentation and tracking**

- Add a short `README.md` to major directories
- Comment **why** a step exists, not only **what** the code does
- Record important thresholds, filters, and random seeds
- Keep a simple analysis catalogue with: analysis ID, purpose, input data, main script, status, and output location

*Small conventions reduce repeated confusion later.*
:::
:::


---

## Best practices that prevent chaos, continued

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Data and output rules**

- Never overwrite raw data
- Keep generated data separate from source data
- Avoid overwriting important outputs
- Create versioned output files when needed
- Use relative paths so the project still works when moved
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Quick checks**

- Can a new person tell what each script is for?
- Can they see which file runs first?
- Can they tell which files are source data and which are generated?
- Can they rerun the project on another machine?

*If not, the structure still needs work.*
:::
:::


---

## Part 4 · Live Walkthrough {background-color="#00326e"}

---

## The UKDRI lab template

**Example repository to review after the session**

```
https://github.com/UKDRI/ukdri-repro-training
```

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Start by checking:**

- The top-level structure
- How the README is written
- The onboarding document
- The project template folder
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**As you review it, ask:**

- Could I find my way around this quickly with no help?
- What would I change for my own lab?
- Which part of this could I copy today?
:::
:::


---

## How to review the example repository

**Top-level README**

- Does it answer: what is this / how do I start / where do I find things?
- Is it short enough to actually read?

. . .

**`docs/onboarding.md`**

- Does it explain conventions, not just tools?
- Would a new starter feel welcomed, not overwhelmed?

. . .

**Project template**

- Does it keep the GIN-Tonic numbered folders?
- Is `03_data/001_rawdata/` clearly marked as read-only?
- Is work organised under `04_data_analysis/`?
- Are environment files easy to find in `07_miscellaneous/`?

. . .

*A good template passes the "new starter" test.*


---

## Part 5 · Practical Adoption {background-color="#00326e"}

---

## Don't refactor everything

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**The worst response to this session is:**

*"My repos are a mess — I need to reorganise everything before I can start."*

. . .

You won't. It's too big. It won't happen.

. . .

**The right response is:**

*"I'll apply this to my next project."*

. . .

Old projects can be improved incrementally — one README at a time, one renamed folder at a time.

But the highest-leverage thing you can do is **start the next project correctly.**
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
**Start here:**

1. Copy the project template folder
2. Write a short README *(even if it's ugly)*
3. Keep the numbered folder structure
4. Treat `03_data/001_rawdata/` as read-only
5. Add environment files early in `07_miscellaneous/`

. . .

*Pick one to do today.*
:::
:::


---

## Before you close a project

**A minimal reproducibility checklist:**

. . .

- [ ] README explains what this is and how to run it
- [ ] All code is in version control
- [ ] `03_data/001_rawdata/` is untouched
- [ ] Analysis steps in `04_data_analysis/` are numbered and commented
- [ ] Software environment is captured in `07_miscellaneous/`
- [ ] Important parameters or random seeds are recorded
- [ ] An analysis catalogue or equivalent project log is up to date

. . .

*If all of these are true, someone else can continue your work.*


---

## The "new starter" test

**Before you close your repository, ask:**

. . .

> Could someone who doesn't know this project find the right place to start quickly — with no help from me?

. . .

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**If yes:**

Your repository is well-organised enough. Carry on.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**If no:**

One of these is missing:

- A README that says how to start
- A clear folder structure
- An onboarding guide for newcomers
:::
:::

. . .

*Use this as a recurring check, not a one-off exercise.*


---

## Where you are now

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**After today you have:**

✅ A mental model for why structure matters

✅ Three principles to apply to any repository

✅ A minimal template you can copy immediately

✅ A single test to know if your repo is "good enough"

. . .

**The next step is yours.**

Copy the template. Write one README.
Start with your next project.
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
**Going further:**

- Add `onboarding.md` to an existing project
- Apply the project template folder to your current analysis
- Run the new starter test on a repo you own

. . .

*You don't need to do all of this at once.*
:::
:::


---

## Resources

**Templates and tools**

- UKDRI repro template
- Cookiecutter Data Science
- Snakemake profiles
- Awesome README
- Make a README

**Reproducibility guides**

- British Ecological Society Guide to Reproducible Code
- rOpenSci Reproducibility Guide
- The Turing Way: Naming files, folders and other things
- Good Enough Practices in Scientific Computing
- GIN-Tonic standard

. . .

*Questions? Drop them in the chat or raise your hand.*


---

## Thank you {background-color="#00326e"}

*Patrícia Rodrigues*
UK Dementia Research Institute
